sections.content:

You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 72, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Trail touches the blue block but blocks don't change. ACTION7 only trims the trail. I've used 70 actions without solving the level. I haven't tried MOUSE yet.
- Plan: Try MOUSE on the red block to see if it changes the block or completes the level.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 71 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
MOUSE on the red block did nothing (gameplay_changed=False). The palette shows the blocks with w/g connectors, not with white interiors. So the palette's red block is RRRR, not RWWR. I was wrong about the palette earlier.
Let me look at the palette more carefully:
Row 58: ccccccccccMWMMWwgRRRRwgNNNNwgbbbbwccccccccccc
Row 59: ccccccccccMWMMWgwRRRRgwNNNNgwbbbbgccccccccccc
The palette shows:
- Agent: MWMMW / MWMMW (pink with white interior)
- Red block: RRRR / RRRR (solid red)
- Green block: NNNN / NNNN (solid green, but N is light green)
- Blue block: bbbb / bbbb (solid blue)
The blocks are connected by w/g pixels.
So the palette shows the target configuration: agent connected to red, green, and blue blocks via w/g trails.
But the panel shows:
- Agent at rows 24-29
- Trail at rows 26-27, touching the blue block
- Red block at rows 19-22, not connected to the trail
- Green block at rows 31-34, not connected to the trail
What if the goal is to connect all three blocks to the agent via the trail? But the trail is horizontal and can only be at one row at a time.
Wait, what if the trail can be vertical too? The vertical strip on the left (cols 13-14) is g/w alternating. What if this is also a trail?
Looking at the vertical strip:
Row 12: g (top)
Row 13: g
Row 14: g
Row 15: g
Row 16: g
Row 17: g
Row 18: g
Row 19: g
Row 20: g
Row 21: g
Row 22: g
Row 23: g
Row 24: g
Row 25: g
Row 26: g
Row 27: g
Row 28: g
Row 29: g
Row 30: g
Row 31: g
Row 32: g
Row 33: g
Row 34: g
Row 35: g
The vertical strip is at cols 13-14, and it's g/g (dark gray). It spans from row 12 to row 35.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the vertical strip is a separate object from the horizontal trail. The horizontal trail is w/g alternating, and the vertical strip is g/g.
What if the vertical strip is also a trail, and it connects the agent to the blocks vertically? But the blocks are at cols 42-45 and 48-51, which are far to the right of the vertical strip.
Hmm, I'm running out of ideas. Let me try a different approach. What if I need to use MOUSE on the palette blocks to "select" them, and then the agent needs to "paint" the corresponding panel block?
Let me try MOUSE on the palette's red block. The palette is at rows 58-59. The red block in the palette is at cols 21-24 (approximately).
Actually, let me look at the palette more carefully:
Row 58: ccccccccccMWMMWwgRRRRwgNNNNwgbbbbwccccccccccc
Row 59: ccccccccccMWMMWgwRRRRgwNNNNgwbbbbgccccccccccc
The palette elements are:
- Agent: cols 14-18 (MWMMW)
- Red block: cols 21-24 (RRRR)
- Green block: cols 27-30 (NNNN)
- Blue block: cols 33-36 (bbbb)
Let me try MOUSE on the palette's red block at row 58, col 22.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: MOUSE on the panel's red block did nothing. The palette shows the target configuration with w/g connectors between agent and blocks. I need to try MOUSE on the palette.
Plan: Try MOUSE on the palette's red block to see if it selects or activates something.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try MOUSE on the palette's red block
# Palette is at rows 58-59
# Red block in palette is at cols 21-24
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 22}])
print(f"MOUSE on palette red: gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check if anything changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'R' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_r = min(p[0] for p in b)
        print(f"  R block at row {min_r}, hash={n['hash']}")

# Check the panel
ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nRow 20: {lines[20][10:55]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
